ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL
ASSEMBLY , WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF FRANK-
FORT , KENTUCKY , ON TUESDAY , JANUARY THE
FIFTH , AND ENDED ON WEDNESDAY , MARCH THE
SEVENTEENTH , NINETEEN HUNDRED TWENTY -SIX ..
CHAPTER 1 .
AN ACT to accept the provisions of an act passed by the Con-
gress of the United States and approved February 24 , 1925 ,
to authorize the more complete endowment of agricultural ex-
periment stations and for other purposes .
Whereas , the Congress of the United States passed an act en-
titled , "An act to authorize the more complete endowment of
agricultural experiment stations , and for other purposes ," ap-
proved February 24 , 1925 ; and
Whereas , by section 2 of the said act the grants of money au
thorized by the said act are made subject to the legislative as-
sent of the several states and territories to the purpose of said
grants : Provided , that payment of such installments of the ap-
propriation herein made as shall become due to any state or
territory before the adjournment of the regular session of the
legislature meeting next after the passage of this act shall be
made upon the assent of the Governor thereof , duly certified by
the Secretary of the Treasury ; and
Whereas , the last legislature of this State had adjourned prior
to February 24 , 1925 ; and
Whereas , the Governor of the State certified to the Secretary
of the Treasury that the legislature was not in session and ac-
cepted the provisions of said act for this State until the meeting
of the next legislature ; therefore ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the legislature of this Commonwealth
assents to the purposes of the grants of the act en-

titled , " An Act to authorize the more complete en-

exist , and this act shall take effect and become a law
from and after its passage and approval .
Approved March 26 , 1926 .

§ 1. Every person , firm or corporation who shall
stand a stud-horse , jack or bull in this Common-
wealth , shall pay as a license tax to the Common-
of Kentucky , for the privilege of standing said stud-
horse , jack or bull , a sum equal to twenty per cent

the animal to be stood , whether the same be for a
season or to insure a get ; provided, however , that
the minimum license for any stud -horse shall be ten
dollars ( $ 10.00 ) and the minimum license for any
jack shall be ten dollars ( $ 10.00 ) and the minimum
license for any bull shall be one dollar ( $ 1.00 ) . And
in addition to said license , each person applying for
the license shall pay to the Commissioner of Agri-
culture of this Commonwealth , the sum of one dollar
($1.00 ) as a registration fee ; said one dollar ($ 1.00 )
to be paid to the county clerk at the time of the
issual of any license for a stud -horse , jack or bull .
The applicant for such license shall state on oath
the largest amount he intends to charge , directly or
indirectly , for the services of the animal , and if in
property or other things , the value thereof, and said
license shall be procured before any service of any
such animal is rendered .

classification.title: An Act relating to forest reserves and taxation thereof.
classification.summary: This act declares the purpose of forest reserves as public in nature and directs the Tax Commissioner to list such lands for taxation at a valuation not exceeding $2.50 per acre for soil and forest growth, as long as they remain classified as forest reserves. Coal and other minerals are taxed as on other state lands. Forest reserves are subject to sale for nonpayment of taxes. Tax valuations can be revised but not raised above the maximum. Taxes collected are paid into the State Treasury to the credit of the Forest Reserve Fund. Counties with forest reserves will have their state tax contributions reduced by the amount of taxes assessed on those reserves. Owners can withdraw land from the act by written application.

32 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
§ 2. Any forest warden duly appointed by the
Department of Agriculture , Labor and Statistics
may arrest , without a warrant , any person or per-
sons taken by him in the act of violating any of the
laws for the protection of the forests of the state .
No action for trespass shall lie against any forest
warden , or person summoned by him, for crossing
or working upon lands of another in connection
with his duties of forest warden . Any forest war-
den may administer an oath or affirmation in order
to examine any person whom he believes knows
facts relating to any forest fire or who claims com-
pensation for services rendered . Any forest war-
den may summon any male resident of the state over
eighteen ( 18 ) years of age who is physically able to
assist in extinguishing forest fires , and may require
the use of horses , automobiles , tools and other equip-
ment needed for such purpose .
§ 3. Any person so summoned , who is physically
able , who refuses , or neglects to so assist , or to al-
low the use of any required equipment or materials ,
shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and liable to a pen-
alty of not less than five dollars ( $5.00 ) nor more
than fifty dollars ( $50.00 ) .
§ 4. Owners of said required equipment and ma-
terials shall receive reasonable compensation there-
for, as determined by the forest warden , or in case
of disagreement upon the terms of compensation ,
the dispute shall be referred to the State Forester
or Assistant State Forester , who shall make the
final decision . Inability or failure to pay persons
summoned to assist in extinguishing forest fires
shall not be a bar to the authority of the forest war-
den to summon such person .
Approved March 25 , 1926 .

weight and fineness , at such place or places as said
Commissioners of the Sinking Fund may determine .
Said bonds may, at the request of the owners , be
registered both as to principal and interest, with the
Auditor of Public Accounts, without payment of any
fee for such registration . All said bonds shall be
exempt from State , county and municipal taxation .

CHAPTER 15 .
AN ACT to repeal , amend and re -enact section 108 of the Ken-
tucky Statutes , Carroll's 1915 edition relating to and prohibit-
ing partnership between certain public officials to practice
law .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That section 108 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
1922 edition , be repealed , amended and re -enacted
so that as amended said section shall read as fol-
lows :
1. No partnership for the purpose of practicing
law shall exist between the clerk of any circuit court
and the clerk of any county court, or any judge of
any court, or police court, or between either of said
officers and any attorney-at-law .
2. Any person violating any of the provisions of
the preceding section shall be guilty of a misdemea-
nor and upon conviction shall be fined not less than
twenty-five nor more than five hundred dollars for
each offense .
3. All laws or parts of laws in conflict herewith
are hereby repealed .
Approved March 24 , 1926 .

to perform the duties so required .

monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section eight hundred sixty-five of Ken-

by Hon . John D. Carroll , and being also section

teen hundred eighteen , be amendeed by striking out

" The moneys accumulated after due allowance
uade for necessary and proper expenses and for
withdrawal of shares , shall at each monthly or
weekly meeting be offered to members according to

laws . Each member , whose application for a loan

strict the maximum amount of loan to which mem-
bers may be entitled . If a balance of money remains
after the weekly or monthly loans are granted the
directors may invest the same in good and safe
bonds or in such other securities in which banks are
now permitted to invest.
§ 2. This Act shall take effect from and after its
passage ."

Criminal Code of Practice , sixth edition , 1919. "
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :

Act to repeal and re -enact section seventy -five of
Carroll's Criminal Code of Practice , sixth edition ,
1919 ," be amended and re -enacted so as to read as

This applies to both regular circuit courts and courts of continuous session, with specific provisions for cases involving nonresident defendants or infant defendants.

mitted in the lower court , and that the judgment

without an opinion , unless the court thinks the
questions involved of sufficient importance to write
an opinion . An order refusing to grant an appeal
will have the same effect as an affirmance of the
judgment, and if the judgment has been superseded ,
damages will follow . No petition for a rehearing
will be allowed if the motion to grant an appeal is
overruled, nor will oral arguments be allowed un-
less the court , after considering the record , decides

gument .

CHAPTER 45 .
AN ACT requiring county judges , police judges , justices of the
peace , and clerks of the circuit courts to keep a record book
in which all judgments , civil and criminal , shall be entered
and cross -indexed , and imposing penalties for failure so to do.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. The county judges , police judges , justices of
the peace, and clerks of the circuit courts of this
Commonwealth shall keep a record called judgment
book , in which shall be entered all judgments , civil
and criminal , and same shall be alphabetically cross-
indexed according to the surnames of each plaintiff
and each defendant . The record so required to be
kept in such judgment book shall show the date of
the judgment , the name of the plaintiff and defend-
ant , the order book and page thereof on which en-
tered , and the date of the satisfaction of said judg-
ment when satisfied .
$ 2. Any county judge , police judge , justice of the
peace , or clerk of the circuit court who shall fail to
record the judgments entered in their respective
courts in the judgment book as herein directed shall
be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor and , upon con-
viction , shall be fined in any sum not exceeding $50.00
and each omission of entry of anything herein re-

CHAPTER 49.
AN ACT to amend and re-enact section 2260 , Kentucky Statutes ,
nineteen hundred and fifteen edition , by Hon . John D. Carroli ,
in relation to compensation of petit jurors in circuit and in
ferior courts .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section 2260 , Kentucky Statutes , nine-
teen hundred and fifteen edition , by Hon . John D.
Carroll , be amended by striking therefrom the word
" Two " where it appears before the word " Dollars ”
in said section , and inserting in lieu thereof the
word " Three , " so that said section when amended
shall read as follows :
Sec . 2260. Jurors in the circuit court shall re-
ceive three dollars ( $ 3.00 ) per day, and when kept
together overnight in custody of the sheriff , shall re-
ceive one dollar ($1.00 ) additional for each night
they are so kept together ; and in courts inferior to
the circuit courts , fifty cents ( $0.50 ) for each case .
In the circuit court a jury fee of four dollars ( $4.00 )
shall be taxed as costs against the unsuccessful
party ; and in the other courts the cost of the jury
shall be paid by the party demanding the jury , and
shall be taxed as costs against the unsuccessful
party ; but in such courts , if the same juror serves
on more than two juries in one day , he shall not be
allowed anything for such excess .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 59 .
AN ACT defining spite fences , providing a penalty for same ,
providing that a court of equity may enjoin such fences and
excepting from the provisions of this act bill boards or fences
used for advertising purposes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Definition . A spite fence is hereby defined
to be a fence , whether a division fence or otherwise ,
and over five feet in height , erected maliciously by
an owner or lessee and intended to injure , spite , or
annoy an adjoining owner .
§ 2. Any person who erects , or maintains such a
fence shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and shall
upon conviction be fined not less than five dollars
nor more than ten dollars for such offense and for
each day the said fence is maintained it shall con-
stitute a separate offense .
$§ 3. A court of equity may enjoin or restrain the
erection or maintenance of such fence .
§ 4. Nothing in this act shall be construed to in-
terfere with the erection or maintenance of bill
boards or fences used for advertising purposes .
Approved March 22 , 1926 .

CHAPTER 61.
AN ACT prohibiting blackmail , defining same , and prescribing
penalties therefor .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That it shall be unlawful for any one to com-
mit blackmail , as that term is defined in this act
§ 2. Any person who , knowing the contents there-
of, and with intent, by means thereof , to extort or
gain any money or other property , or to do , abet or
procure any illegal or wrongful act , sends , delivers ,
or in any manner causes to be forwarded or re-
ceived , or makes or parts with for the purpose that
there may be sent or delivered , any letter or writ-
ing , threatening :
a. To accuse any person of a crime ; or ,
b. To do any injury to any person or to any prop-
erty ; or
or
C. To publish or connive at publishing any libel ;
d . To expose or impute to any person any de-
formity or disgrace ,
Shall be guilty of a felony , and shall be punished
by confinement in the State reformatory for not less
than one, nor more than ten years .
§ 3. Any person who , under circumstances not
amounting to robbery or an attempted robbery, with
intent to extort any gain or property , orally makes
such a threat as would be a violation of the forego-
ing section if made or enacted in writing , shall be
guilty of a misdemeanor , and shall be fined for each
conviction not less than one hundred nor more than
five hundred dollars , and confined in the county jail
not less than thirty nor more than one hundred and
twenty days , in the discretion of the jury .

classification.summary: This act amends Section 1321 of the Kentucky Statutes to clarify that amateur sports and athletic games are not considered 'work, labor, trade, business or calling' for the purposes of prohibitions against doing business on the Sabbath day. The original section prohibited work or business on the Sabbath, with exceptions for household offices, necessity, charity, and maintenance of certain transportation services.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 183
�
ate offense . Persons who are members of a religi-
ous society , who observe as a Sabbath any other day
in the week than Sunday, shall not be liable to the
penalty prescribed in this section , if they observe
as a Sabbath one day in each seven , as herein pro-
vided. Provided that amateur sports and athletic
games shall not be considered a work , labor , trade ,
business or calling within the meaning of this sec-
tion .
Disapproved March 17 , 1926 .
Attached to the foregoing act are the following certificates of
the clerks of the Senate and House of Representatives , re-
spectively :
IN SENATE
March 17 , 1926
A message was received from the Governor that
he had disapproved a bill which originated in the
Senate of the following title :
S. B. No. 332. An act to amend section 1321 of the Kentucky
Statutes , Carroll's 1915 edition .
Said message reads as follows :
To the President of the Senate .
My dear Mr. President :
I return herewith with my disapproval Senate bill number
332 .
This bill if enacted into law would , in my opinion , in many
instances prove a great disadvantage to churches and Sunday
schools , particularly in rural sections , where baseball and other
sports which would come within the purview of this act , could
and doubtless would be conducted in close proximity to the
church during hours of worship , as a result of which the pro-
gress of the latter would be impeded .
Furthermore the fourth commandment is just as obligatory as
any of the ten given from Mount Sinai , hence I would not feel
justified in approving any measure inimical to a proper observ .
ance of the Sabbath day .
A Christian civilization cannot subordinate its interest in the
church and kindred institutions to its interest in sports and long
survive . I therefore feel impelled to disapprove the bill .
Signed : W. J. FIELDS , Governor .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 230

classification.summary: This act, which was disapproved by the Governor, proposed to amend a section of the Kentucky Statutes related to Sabbath observance. It would have exempted members of religious societies observing a Sabbath other than Sunday, provided they observed one day in seven. It also specified that amateur sports and athletic games would not be considered work or labor. The Governor's disapproval was based on concerns that allowing sports near churches during worship hours would impede religious services and that the measure was inimical to the proper observance of the Sabbath day.

classification.summary: This statute establishes the Kentucky Crippled Children Commission, composed of seven members appointed by the Governor. The commission can appoint a director and staff.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 197
§ 147. The General Assembly shall provide by law
for the registration of all persons entitled to vote
in cities and towns having a population of five thou-
sand or more ; and may provide by general law for
the registration of other voters in the State . Where
registration is required, only persons registered
shall have the right to vote . The mode of registration
shall be prescribed by the General Assembly . In all
elections by persons in a representative capacity ,
the voting shall be viva voce and made a matter of
record ; but all elections by the people shall be by
secret official ballot , furnished by public authority
to the voters at the polls , and marked by each voter
in private at the polls , and then and there deposited .
Provided , however , that the General Assembly may
enact laws to permit electors or voters who are un-
avoidably absent , or who are required by their regu-
lar business or occupation to be absent , from their
voting precincts on the day of the election , to exer-
cise the right of suffrage and to vote in all elections
held within their precincts , and to provide all neces-
sary means for preparing , delivering and having
counted such votes . The word " Elections " in this
section includes the decision of questions submitted
to the voters , as well as the choice of officers by them .
The first General Assembly held after the adoption
of this Constitution shall pass all necessary laws to
enforce this provision , and shall provide that per-
sons illiterate , bind , or in any way disabled , may
have their ballots marked as herein required .
§ 2. That this amendment shall be submitted to
the voters of the State for their ratification or rejec-
tion at the time and in the manner provided for
under section 256 of the Constitution and under sec-
tion 1459 Kentucky Statutes .
Approved March 19 , 1926 .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 244

classification.title: Amendment to the Constitution Regarding Voter Registration and Voting Procedures
classification.summary: This act proposes an amendment to the Kentucky Constitution concerning voter registration in cities and towns with populations of 5,000 or more, and allows for general registration laws for other voters. It mandates secret official ballots for popular elections and outlines provisions for absent and disabled voters. The amendment was to be submitted for ratification.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses voter registration and voting procedures. While it does not explicitly mention race, voter registration laws during the Jim Crow era were often used as a tool to disenfranchise Black voters through discriminatory practices like poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses, even if these were not explicitly stated in the law itself. The mention of providing for illiterate, blind, or disabled voters could be interpreted in the context of Jim Crow practices, where such provisions were sometimes used to circumvent discriminatory intent or, conversely, to create loopholes for disenfranchisement. Because the statute's impact on racial groups is not explicit and requires historical context to fully understand its potential discriminatory application, it is classified as 'ambiguous' and requires human review.

CHAPTER 72.
AN ACT directing the submission of an amendment to section
246 of the Constitution of Kentucky , relating to the compensa .
tion of public officers .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. There shall be submitted to the qualified
voters of Kentucky , for their approval or disappro-
val , at the time and in the manner provided by the
Constitution and the laws of this Commonwealth ,
the following as an amendment to the present Con-
stitution of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , to-
wit :
That section 246 of the present Constitution of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky be repealed and
stricken therefrom , and that in lieu thereof it shall
be provided , under the same section number , as fol-
lows :
Section 246. The General Assembly shall have
power and it shall be its duty to fix reasonable com-
pensation for all public officers , and the method or
methods of paying such compensation .
The provisions of this section shall be made ap-
plicable to incumbents in office at the time of the
ratification of this amendment and at the time the
General Assembly shall first after its adoption fix
the salaries of public officers affected thereby .
Approved March 25 , 1926 .

CHAPTER 74 .
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation , providing for cor
poration license tax for certain corporations , providing the
manner of making reports and of determining the amount of
tax and the manner of paying same , prescribing penalties and
repealing existing statutes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
1. License Tax on Corporations . Every cor-
poration organized by or under the laws of this or
any other State or Government , owning property or
doing business in this State , except foreign insur-
ance companies , whether fire , life , accident , casualty ,
or indemnity , foreign and domestic building and
loan associations , banks and trust companies , and
corporations which , under existing laws , are liable
to pay a franchise tax, shall pay to this State , to
be credited to the general expenditure fund , an an-
nual license tax based upon the asset value of its
capital stock , as hereinafter provided .
§ 2. Amount of Tax . Every corporation shall
pay an annual license tax of seventy cents on each
one thousand dollars of that part of the asset value
of its capital stock represented by property owned
and business transacted in this State , which shall be
ascertained by finding the proportion that the prop-
erty owned and business transacted in this State
bears to the aggregate amount of property owned
and business transacted in and out of this State.
Provided , that such corporation may pay at said
rate upon the entire asset value of its capital stock ;
and in that event it shall not be required to report
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 250

classification.title: AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation, providing for corporation license tax for certain corporations, providing the manner of making reports and of determining the amount of tax and the manner of paying same, prescribing penalties and repealing existing statutes.
classification.summary: This act imposes an annual license tax on corporations organized in or out of state that own property or conduct business in Kentucky. The tax is based on the asset value of the corporation's capital stock, specifically the portion represented by property and business within the state. Certain entities like foreign insurance companies, building and loan associations, banks, trust companies, and corporations already liable for a franchise tax are exempt. The tax rate is seventy cents per one thousand dollars of the relevant asset value.

classification.summary: This act repeals and replaces Section 4077 of the Kentucky Statutes (1915 edition). The new section mandates that various companies, including railway, gas, water, ferry, bridge, street railway, express, electric light, electric power, telegraph, press dispatch, telephone, bus line, palace car, dining car, sleeping car, chair car, tank car, coal car, and pipe line companies, as well as any other corporation or association exercising special privileges or performing public services, must annually pay a tax on their franchise to the state and local taxing districts. The State Tax Commission is designated as the board of valuation and assessment for these franchises.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 2 of the Kentucky Securities Act
classification.summary: This act amends the Kentucky Securities Act by adding exemptions to the registration requirements for certain securities transactions. These exemptions include sales to financial institutions, corporations for their own account, dealers who qualify securities, transfers in connection with corporate consolidations or mergers, bonds secured by real estate sold to a single purchaser, securities outstanding for at least 18 months with no default history, and the sale of securities by railroad or public utility corporations to their employees.

§ 6. Registration by Notification . The follow-
ing classes of securities shall be entitled to registra-
tion by notification in the manner provided in this
section :
( 1 ) Securities issued by a corporation , part-
nership , association , company , syndicate or trust
owning a property , business or industry which has
been in continuous operation not less than three
years or which is a consolidation or merger of a
property , business or industry which has been in con-
tinuous operation for not less than three years , and
which has shown during a period of not less than
two years or more than ten years next prior to the
close of its last fiscal year preceding the offering of
such securities , average annual net earnings , after
deducting all prior charges not including the charges
upon securities to be retired out of the proceeds of
sale , as follows :
(a ) In the case of interest bearing securities ,
not less than one and one-half times the annual inter-
est charge thereon and upon all other outstanding
interest bearing obligations of equal rank .
(b ) In the case of preferred stock , not less
than one and one -half times the annual dividend re-
quirements on such preferred stock and on all other
outstanding stock of equal or senior rank .

§ 7. Registration by Qualification . All secur-
ities required by this act to be registered before be-
ing sold in this State , and not entitled to registration
by notification, shall be registered only by qualifi-
cation in the manner provided by this section .
The commissioner shall receive and act upon ap-
plications to have securities registered by qualifi-
cation , and may prescribe forms on which he may
require such applications to be submitted . Appli-
cations shall be in writing and shall be duly signed
and sworn to by the applicant , and filed in the office
of the commissioner and may be made either by the
issuer of the securities for which registration is ap-
plied or by any registered dealer desiring to sell the
same within this State .
The commissioner shall require the applicant
to submit to the commissioner the following infor-
mation respecting the issuer and such other informa-
tion as he may in his judgment deem necessary to
enable him to ascertain whether such securities shall
be registered pursuant to the provisions of this
section :
(a) The names and addresses of the directors ,
trustees and officers , if the issuer be a corporation
or association or trust organized or existing under
the common law ( as hereinbefore defined ) , of all
partners, if the issuer be a partnership , and of the
issuer, if the issuer be an individual .
(b) The location of issuer's principal business
office and of its principal office in this State , if any .
(c) The purpose of incorporation (if incorpor-
ated ) and the general character of business actually
to be transacted by the issuer , and the purpose of
the proposed issue .

classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for registering securities for sale in Kentucky. ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 247
§ 34. Penalty for Sale of Securities of Insol-
vent Issuer . Any issuer or agent , salesman , dealer
or broker of or for any issuer, knowing such issuer
to be insolvent , who shall offer to sell any securities
issued by such issuer, or who shall make any sale of
any such securities of and for such issuer , shall be
deemed guilty of a felony, and upon conviction
thereof in any court of competent jurisdiction , shall
be punished by confinement in the penitentiary for
not less than one nor more than ten years . For the
purpose of this section an issuer shall be deemed in-
solvent whenever the aggregate of his or its prop-
erty shall not , at a fair valuation , be sufficient in
amount to pay his or its debts .
§ 35. Penalty for Violations of this Act . Any
person who wilfully does , or aids or advises in doing
any act that is not included in and punished by sec-
tions 31 , 32 , 33 and 34 of this act , and that is pro-
hibited or made unlawful by any of the provisions of
this act , shall be guilty of a felony , and upon convic-
tion thereof , shall be imprisoned in the penitentiary
for not less than one nor more than seven years .
$ 36. Constitutionality . Should the court de-
clare any section or clause of this act unconstitu-
tional , then such decision shall affect only the section
or clause so declared to be unconstitutional and shall
not affect any other section or clause of this act .
This act shall take effect when it is approved by
the Governor and under and according to section 55
of the Constitution , and when it does take effect the
act that was enacted by the legislature at its 1920
session , and that is chapter 125 , page 582 of the Ses-
sion Acts of 1920 (now section 883e - 1 of Carroll's
Kentucky Statutes , 1922 edition ) , and known as the
"Kentucky Blue Sky Law " shall be and the same is
hereby repealed .
Approved March 23 , 1926 .

CHAPTER 79
AN ACT repealing section 4465b , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
edition 1915 , Baldwin's Supplement 1918 , relating to the man-

enacting in lieu thereof the following providing for such elec-

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That section 4465b , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
1915 edition , Baldwin's 1918 supplement , be and the
same is hereby repealed and in lieu thereof the fol-
lowing is enacted :
All school elections in graded school districts
shall be by secret ballot . Such ballots shall be on a
separate sheet from all other ballots to be used in
any election . In elections for board members in
such graded school districts it shall be the duty of
the county clerk of any county in which a graded
school district is situated to cause to be printed on
such graded school ballots , the names of all candi-
dates for membership on such board of education
in whose behalf he shall be petitioned so to do , in
writing by not less than ten ( 10 ) legal voters of such
district . The petition must be filed in the office of
the county clerk not more than sixty and not less
than fifteen days prior to the day of election , and
each petition shall show the place of residence of
each person signing it, and no person shall sign
more petitions than the number of offices to be filled .

the General Assembly of 1924 , be repealed , and that

2. Examination Questions . It shall be the
duty of the State Board of Examiners to prepare
annually two sets of questions covering the subject
matter of the elementary course of study and the

one to begin on the fourth Thursday of May and the

within ten days before any examination date for-
ward by registered mail a reasonable number of
question lists for such examination to all county su-
perintendents entitled to receive them . The ques-
tions on each subject shall be enclosed in a separate
properly labeled envelope , and all enclosed in one
package , which package shall be securely sealed ,
plainly addressed , and marked to show the nature of
content and date of examination for which intended .
The county superintendent shall preserve the pack-
age unopened until the hour of examination , when it
shall be scrutinized by the examiner or examiners
and applicants for certificate , after which it shall be
opened by the superintendent in their presence . The
superintendent and examiner or examiners in charge
shall each under official oath and on forms furnished
for that purpose by the Superintendent of Public
Instruction , make separate certification that he in-
spected the package and whether or not it was found
intact, and shall on that same date forward same to
the director of certification .

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 305
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That chapter 33 of the Acts of 1902 , being entitled
" An act to change the name of the State Normal
School for Colored Persons , and to regulate the con-
duct of same , " be and the same is now repealed , and
re -enacted , and as re -enacted shall read as follows ,
to -wit :
That the name of the State Normal School for
Colored Persons is hereby changed to the following ,
to -wit : " Kentucky State Industrial College for Col-
ored Persons , " and the board of trustees shall be
known as the Board of Trustees of Kentucky State
Industrial College for Colored Persons .
That the president of the college , who shall be
styled the " President of the College , " and shall be
the chief administrative officer of the college under
control of the board of trustees . The president shall
be elected during the month of June , 1926 , and shall
be elected by the board for a term of four years .
That the Kentucky State Industrial College for Col-
ored Persons shall have the right to certificate any
graduate of its institution to teach any branch of
school work in any colored school in the State of
Kentucky , and any person holding such certificate
shall not be required to pass any further examina-
tion before any school authorities of the State .
Whereas , the school terms always close in June
of each year, and arrangements for the conduct of
the college , including the employment of teachers
for the succeeding school year , can be more advan-
tageously done and arranged before the end of the
school term , it is considered that an emergency
exists for this law , and it shall be and become a law
upon its pasage and approval by the Governor of
the Commonwealth .
Approved March 25 , 1926 .

§ 2. Section 1954c - 25 . Non- resident or alien
hunter's license ; how obtained .-Any non - resident
or alien of this state , or person who has not been a
resident of this state for one year , may procure a
license for hunting by filing his or her affidavit with
the county clerk of any county in this state , stating
his or her age , place of residence , post office address ,
height, color, color of his or her eyes , and hair , and
the fact whether he or she can write her name , and
by paying to said county clerk the sum of twenty-
five dollars ($25.00 ) , which license shall entitle him
or her to hunt in all the counties of this state , after
receiving the permission of the owner or agent of
the land .
§ 3. Nineteen hundred fifty-four c-twenty-four
whenever used in the Fish and Game protective laws
unless a contrary intention is evident from the con-
text the word " hunt " shall intend taking , killing ,
trapping , injuring or pursuing with intent to take ,
kill or injure and shall also include chasing with
dogs , regardless of intent ; however , nothing in this
act shall be construed to prevent any non -resident
from participating in any regularly advertised field
trial without procuring a license .

·
2. The State Game and Fish Commission may
upon application issue a license to take , catch or kill
mussels . On making application for such license
the residents of this State shall pay to the State
Game and Fish Commission a fee of $10.00 , and
non -residents shall pay to such Game and Fish Com-
mission a fee of $ 50.00 . All such licenses shall ex-
pire on the 31st day of December following their
issue . The licenses shall be consecutively num-
bered as issued and record shall be kept thereof in
the office of the State Game and Fish Commission .
Such license shall state whether it is a resident or
non -resident license and it shall entitle the holder
thereof to operate one boat only. An operator de-
siring to operate more than one boat shall be re-
quired to secure a separate license for each boat .
The holder of a license while taking, catching or kill-
ing mussels for commercial purposes shall have his
license with him ready for exhibition and shall ex-
hibit the same when required to do so by an author-
ized officer .
§ 3. The license provided for herein shall entitle
the holder thereof to operate on any shell bed , Pro-
vided not more than four boats may operate to the
mile of shell beds .
§ 4. On or before the 31st day of December of the
year in which any license is issued , the holder there-
of shall make a written report to the State Game
and Fish Commission on blanks furnished by the
Commission for the purpose of stating the total
weight of mussels taken , caught or killed under such
license , the names and location of the waters from
which such mussels were taken and the amount re-
ceived for shells sold . Upon failure to make such
report the State Game and Fish Commission shall
not issue another license until such report shall be
made .

CHAPTER 96.
AN ACT for the protection of homing and carrier pigeons .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
First . It shall be unlawful for any person to
shoot , trap or in any way injure a homing or carrier
pigeon , whether in flight or at rest . Any person or
persons violating this act upon conviction shall be
fined not less than ten ( $10.00 ) or more than twenty-
five ( $25.00 ) or be imprisoned for a term of not less
than ten nor more than sixty days .

It specifically addresses the investment of capital and accumulations in home office buildings, allowing companies organized before 1918 and those organized after to invest in their home office buildings under certain conditions and with the approval of the insurance commissioner. It also declares an emergency to address an inequality between older and newer companies regarding these investment permissions. The act also pertains to the taxation of domestic life insurance companies, including the rate on shares of stock and how their value is determined.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses regulations for domestic life insurance companies, specifically regarding their investments in home office buildings and taxation. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The statute appears to be purely financial and regulatory in nature. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned.

classification.title: An Act relating to the taxation of life insurance companies.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's edition 1922, sections 4237a (1) and (2), concerning the taxation of life insurance companies. It clarifies that the net cash value or reserve of outstanding policies is considered the property of policyholders and not subject to listing for taxation by domestic life insurance companies. Instead, domestic companies are to pay an annual tax of 50 cents per one hundred dollars of the fair cash value of their shares of stock for state purposes only. This tax is in lieu of all other state, county, city, town, or other taxing district taxes, except that companies must still list and pay taxes on their real estate located within these districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the taxation of life insurance companies and their assets. It specifies how the net cash value of policies and the value of company shares are to be taxed, and clarifies that this tax is for state purposes and in lieu of other local taxes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is purely on financial and tax regulations for insurance companies. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to convey a portion of the Zachary Taylor burial grounds to the United States for a national cemetery.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Governor of Kentucky to appoint a commissioner to convey a portion of the Zachary Taylor burial grounds in Jefferson County, Kentucky, owned by the Commonwealth, to the United States of America for the establishment of a national cemetery. The conveyance is to be made free of cost to the United States, in accordance with an act of Congress. The commissioner is empowered to survey the land and include a description in the deed. The act also includes a proviso regarding the future use of the land by the United States government.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 1 of an Act Entitled "An Act to Create the Kentucky Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board and to Define Its Powers and Duties," Approved March 14, 1924, and to Extend the Existence of Said Board.
classification.summary: This act extends the existence of the Kentucky Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board and clarifies its composition and duties. The board is tasked with assisting disabled veterans in filing claims with federal agencies, expediting action on these claims, and ensuring veterans receive their entitled relief. The board will consist of the Lieutenant Governor, the State Commander of the American Legion, Department of Kentucky, and a third member elected by these two. The act defines "ex-service men and women" as individuals who served on active duty in the U.S. Army, Navy, or Marine Corps between April 6, 1917, and July 2, 1921, and received an honorable discharge. A secretary will be elected to manage claims and maintain contact with the U.S. Veterans' Bureau.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the establishment and duties of a board to assist disabled ex-service men and women with their claims to federal agencies. The text does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race. The focus is solely on veteran status and disability. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1926. The confidence is high because the statute's purpose is clearly defined and has no apparent racial component.

classification.title: An Act Relating to Automobile Dealers' Plates

A verified statement of eligible individuals must be filed with the clerk.

classification.title: An Act relating to the registration of automobiles and the use of "In transit" plates.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the regulations for "In transit" plates issued to automobile manufacturers and dealers in Kentucky. It specifies that these plates are for transporting vehicles from the place of manufacture or wholesale to the dealer's place of business and prohibits their use for demonstration or commercial activities. The act details the process for returning plates, forfeiture of deposits for late returns, and record-keeping requirements for county court clerks. It also establishes penalties for violations, classifying them as misdemeanors with fines ranging from ten to one hundred dollars. The provisions were to become effective on December 1, 1926. Additionally, it mentions the application process for registration of automobiles owned by government entities or educational boards.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of "In transit" license plates for automobile manufacturers and dealers. It details the permitted uses of these plates, the procedures for their return, and penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a purely administrative and regulatory measure related to vehicle registration. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

mitted on the return trip to haul any person or per-
sons other than those included in the original trip .
§ 22. No motor vehicle engaged in transporta-
tion of persons for hire shall be permitted by its
operator to carry a greater number of passengers
than twenty -five per cent over its seating capacity ,
or the number specified in the certificate issued by
the commisioner . No passenger shall be permitted
to ride upon the steps or the running board of any
motor vehicle engaged in the public transportation
of passengers for hire , nor upon the top of any mo-
tor vehicle engaged in the public transportation of
passengers , unless such top has been designed and
constructed for such use , and is properly provided
with seats and protecting railings , and authorized
by certificate issued by the commissioner . No mo-
tor vehicle shall be operated between fixed termini
or over a regular route with an extreme width ex-
ceeding ninety ( 90 ) inches .
$ 23. Immediate notice , in writing , shall be given
to the commissioner of all accidents , which shall re-
sult in the loss of life or when the injury is of a seri-
ous nature .

CHAPTER 114 .
AN ACT to require any driver of any vehicle on a public high
way, on approaching railway and interurban grade crossings ,
which have been designated as "unsafe " by the State High-
way Commission , to stop , look and listen , before passing
thereover , and to require highway commissioners to erect and
maintain stop signs for such crossings , and to require railway
and interurban companies to furnish and deliver said signs ;
and providing penalties for non -compliance to stop , look and
listen .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That wherever in this State the main
track or tracks of any railroad or any interurban
railway shall cross at grade a public highway, the
State Highway Commission may designate such
crossing as " unsafe ," and it shall be unlawful for
the driver of any vehicle to cross such crossing with-
out first bringing such vehicle to a full stop at not
less than ten ( 10 ) feet , nor more than thirty ( 30 )
feet from the nearest rail of such track or tracks .
The phrase " main track or tracks , " as used in this
section , shall mean any railroad or interurban track
or tracks over which regularly scheduled trains or
cars are operated .
Section 2. That at such crossings as are desig-
rated " unsafe " the State Highway Commission
shall place and maintain on each side of the track or
tracks , on the right side of the highway , not more
than one hundred feet from said track or tracks , a
sign board not less than ( 6 ) feet from the ground ,
eighteen ( 18 ) inches by thirty -six ( 36 ) inches , with
the inscription " Stop- Kentucky Law. "
Section 3. That railroad or interurban compan-
ies shall furnish and deliver at crossings so desig-
nated by said State Highway Commission as " un-

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact certain sections of the Kentucky Statutes relating to public finance, law departments, and police courts.
classification.summary: This act revises and re-enacts several sections of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the offices of auditor, treasurer, city attorney, and police court officials. It abolishes the offices of auditor and treasurer at the expiration of the current incumbents' terms, transferring their duties to an appointed accountant and the director of finance, respectively. It also reorganizes city law departments, abolishing the offices of city attorney and assistants, and imposes their duties on the new department. Furthermore, it revises police court statutes, abolishes the office of prosecuting attorney, and changes the status of the clerk of the police court from elective to appointive. It also addresses confinement in workhouses or county jails and payment of fines by work.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative and organizational changes within Kentucky's government, specifically concerning financial offices, city law departments, and police courts. It focuses on the abolition and restructuring of various official positions and the transfer of their duties. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The changes appear to be purely administrative and procedural. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY'. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Repeal and Reenactment of Sections 2756 and 2765 of Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This statute repeals and reenacts sections 2756 and 2765 of the Kentucky Statutes. Section 2756, as reenacted, grants legislative bodies the power to prescribe duties, terms of office, compensation, bonds, and election/appointment times for city officers and agents. Section 2765, as reenacted, outlines the structure of the legislative body for cities of the first class, initially a board of twenty-four councilmen and twelve aldermen, but transitioning to a single legislative body of twelve members styled the board of aldermen after the 1929 elections.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 2766 and 2767 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922) relating to city councilmen and aldermen.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Sections 2766 and 2767. It redefines how city councilmen and aldermen are elected and structured. Specifically, it states that councilmen shall be elected at large, with two residents from each ward, and that the board of twenty-four councilmen will cease to exist after the expiration of terms for those elected in November 1927. Similarly, aldermen are to be elected at large, and their board will cease to exist after the terms of those elected in November 1927 expire. The act also addresses the transfer of powers and duties from existing general councils to new legislative bodies.

§ 62. That section 2833a of the Kentucky Stat-
utes ( Carroll's edition , 1922 ) , be and the same is
hereby repealed , and in lieu thereof, that said sec-
tion be re -enacted so as to read as follows :
That when any public way, or other public im-
provement in any city of the first class in this Com-
monwealth , is ordered or directed , by ordinance of
the legislative body of such city to be constructed ,
which , according to the provisions of the act for the
government of that class of cities , may be lawfully
constructed at the cost of the owners of the lots of
ground adjacent to such improvement , or within the
taxable limits therefor defined as provided in such
act and any such real estate within such taxable
limits is owned by the State of Kentucky , or is held
in trust for the public use of the State , the propor-
tionate part of the cost of making such public way
or other public improvement shall be apportioned
against the real estate of the State in like manner as
against other lots of ground within such taxable
limits , and apportionment warrant or statement
thereof shall be certified by the board of public works
so long as said board exists , and when said board
shall have ceased to exist , by the director of works
of such city to the Auditor of Public Accounts , who
shall thereupon draw his warrant upon the State
Treasurer for the amount of such apportionment
warrant or certified statement in favor of the person
named therein as entitled to the amount thereof , and
the State Treasurer shall pay said warrant drawn
by the Auditor out of any money in the Treasury
not otherwise appropriated .

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 2872a-13 and Section 2873 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922)
classification.summary: This act amends two sections of the Kentucky Statutes. Section 92 repeals and re-enacts Section 2872a-13, detailing the process for drawing warrants on a city treasurer for pension funds, payable to the treasurer of a board of trustees. Section 93 amends Section 2873, granting the board of public safety authority to prescribe rules, orders, and regulations for the government, administration, disposition, and discipline of the police department and police force, in conformity with laws governing cities of the first class. classification.title: An Act to amend Section 2875 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922)

It also details provisions for the admissibility of certified copies of rules and regulations from the board of public safety, the director of safety, and the civil service board as evidence in legal proceedings. classification.title: Repeal and Reenactment of Sections 2877 and 2878 of Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This statute repeals and reenacts sections 2877 and 2878 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922). Section 2877, as reenacted, authorizes the board of public safety (or department of public safety) to establish and furnish police stations, temporary detention facilities for arrested persons, and necessary business accommodations for the police department. It also empowers them to furnish patrol wagons for the exclusive use of the police department. Section 2878, as reenacted, allows the board of public safety (or department of public safety) to establish a mounted patrol in rural areas, procure horses and equipment, and use teams and vehicles as needed. It also permits the sale of unneeded or obsolete property.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the organization and equipment of the police force, including the establishment of stations, detention facilities, and patrol vehicles. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would inherently discriminate based on race. The language is administrative and operational, focusing on the infrastructure and resources of the police department. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 2881 and 2882 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922) relating to the police force.
classification.summary: This act amends sections 2881 and 2882 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the promotion and disciplinary actions for members of the police force. Section 2882 grants the board of public safety the power to punish members for various offenses, including legal or criminal offenses, neglect of duty, violation of rules, absence without leave, conduct injurious to public peace or welfare, immoral conduct, or other breaches of discipline, with punishments ranging from reprimand to dismissal. These provisions are contingent on the existence of the board of public safety.

or officer of said force on account of any political

officer or member of said force be removed or dis-
charged or reduced in grade or pay for any political
reasons . Their appointment and continuance upon
the police force shall depend absolutely upon their
ability and their willingness to enforce the law.

safety shall have ceased to exist , as provided herein ,
the foregoing provisions shall cease to have effect

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 2893 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922) and Section 2894 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922)
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes sections 2893 and 2894, which pertain to the qualifications and appointment of police officers. Section 2893 is revised to require police members and officers to be electors, residents for three years, of sobriety and integrity, orderly and law-abiding citizens, and not convicted of felonies or engaged in unlawful callings. It also prohibits appointments based on political service and removals for political reasons, emphasizing ability and willingness to enforce the law. Section 2894 is repealed and re-enacted to include private, special, and substitute policemen in the requirements before entering their duties.

It also amends Section 2896a-8 of the Kentucky Statutes to allow the board of public safety to increase the number of firemen upon recommendation of the chief.

classification.summary: This act abolishes the office of auditor in cities of the first class and creates the office of accountant, effective from the election and qualification of a mayor in 1929. classification.title: An Act relating to the finance of cities of the first class
classification.summary: This act details the restructuring of financial management in cities of the first class. It abolishes the elected treasurer position and transfers all powers, obligations, and duties to a newly established department of public finance, headed by a director of finance. This department will oversee all financial matters of the city, except for those of the board of sinking fund commissioners and the purchase of supplies, unless otherwise specified by ordinance. The director will also have the authority to organize the department into divisions and appoint division heads.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 2940 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922)
classification.summary: This act amends Section 2940 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the powers and duties of the bailiff of the police court. Initially, the bailiff had the powers of a constable and policeman. However, the amendment stipulates that after the current bailiff's term expires, this provision will be repealed. Instead, the chief of the division of police in cities of the first class will assign a policeman or policemen to the police court to maintain order and perform other duties. These assigned police officers will retain their existing powers as members of the police department and also assume the powers, obligations, and duties formerly vested in the bailiff, including the power of a constable.

§ 162. That section 3000 of the Kentucky Stat-

The tax receiver shall , as early in the month of

classification.title: Repeal and Reenactment of Section 3003 of Kentucky Statutes Regarding Tax Sales
classification.summary: This statute repeals and reenacts Section 3003 of the Kentucky Statutes. The reenacted section mandates that tax receivers report the results of tax sales to the comptroller (or director of finance when the comptroller's office ceases to exist). The report must include details such as the number and amount of tax bills, date of sale, amount bid, and purchaser's name. The tax receiver is also required to maintain a record of these sales in an indexed book, noting redemptions or conveyances. Upon purchase of realty, the tax receiver shall issue a certificate to the purchaser, detailing the property and the amount paid.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 3004 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922) relating to the redemption of property sold for taxes.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 3004 to clarify the process for redeeming land sold for taxes. It specifies that if land is not redeemed within two years from the date of sale, a fee simple title vests in the purchaser, subject only to state taxes. The tax receiver will then convey the property by deed. Purchasers other than the city must pay all later city tax bills assessed against the property after the tax for which it was sold, with interest, before receiving the deed.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 3037c-2 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's edition, 1922), relating to tuberculosis hospital boards.
classification.summary: This act repeals and re-enacts Section 3037c-2 of the Kentucky Statutes. It modifies the appointment and composition of tuberculosis hospital boards in cities of the first class. Initially, members are appointed by the mayor and serve without compensation, with the mayor as an ex-officio member. Upon the creation of a department of public health and the appointment of its director, the tuberculosis hospital board will cooperate with this department. Subsequently, the mayor will cease to be an ex-officio member, and the director of health will become one. After the director's qualification and upon the expiration of existing board members' terms, the director of health, with the mayor's approval, will appoint new board members who will also serve without compensation.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative structure and appointment process for tuberculosis hospital boards in cities of the first class. It details changes in who appoints board members and who serves ex-officio based on the establishment of a public health department. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the roles of the board of plumbing examiners and the city's general council or board of trustees in creating, amending, and adopting these regulations. It also specifies procedures for cities of the first class, particularly concerning the establishment of a department of public health and the appointment of a director of health.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the regulation of plumbing and drainage within cities, including the establishment of codes and the roles of various governing bodies. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on public health and infrastructure management. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is null as it does not pertain to any of the listed Jim Crow categories. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY'.

classification.title: An act to amend section 3037f, Carroll's edition of Kentucky Statutes, 1922, relating to the registration of plumbers and the supervision and inspection of plumbing and drainage in the cities of the first class, making it apply to cities of the second class as well as the first.
classification.summary: This statute amends existing Kentucky Statutes concerning plumbing inspection and registration. It extends the application of regulations for plumbing inspectors and deputy inspectors to cities of the second class, in addition to cities of the first class. The mayor appoints these inspectors, with approval from the legislative body, for one-year terms.

classification.title: An Act relating to the government of cities of the first class.
classification.summary: This act repeals and re-enacts Section 3037g-80 of the Kentucky Statutes, concerning disagreements between health and building departments in cities of the first class. It establishes a process for resolving such disputes, first by referring them to the board of public safety, and then to the mayor if the board ceases to exist. It also repeals inconsistent sections of Article II of Chapter 89 of the Kentucky Statutes and any conflicting city ordinances.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 573
§ 2. Kentucky Bridge Commission . That the
mayor of any city of the first class , with the approval
of the board of aldermen , may appoint four persons ,
who , with the mayor ex- officio , shall constitute a
bridge commission . Each appointee shall be at
least twenty-five years of age and reside within the
city . No officer or employee of said city, whether
holding a paid or unpaid office , shall be eligible to
hold an appointment on said commission . Two of
such appointees , each of opposite political affiliation ,
shall be originally appointed for terms of four years ,
and two , each of opposite political affiliation , shall
be originally appointed for terms of four years .
Upon the expiration of such terms , subsequent ap-
pointments , made in the same manner , shall be for
four year terms only. Not more than two of such
appointees shall be members of the same dominant
political party . Vacancies shall be filled for any
unexpired term in the same manner as the original
appointment , provided however that the terms of
all said members shall cease if such commission be-
comes dissolved as hereinafter provided by this act .
The persons appointed as provided herein shall
constitute a body corporate under the name of ( in-
sert name of city ) bridge commission and shall have
power to contract and be contracted with , to sue and
be sued in that name and to adopt a seal and alter
same at pleasure . Said commission shall elect a
chairman from its members , and a secretary and
treasurer who need not be a member of such comi
mission . The chairman shall receive a salary not
exceeding five thousand dollars ($ 5,000.00 ) per an-
num , and the secretary and treasurer shall receive
a salary not exceeding five thousand dollars
( $5,000.00 ) per annum . Such salaries shall be fixed
by the general council within the above limitations.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 620

classification.title: Kentucky Bridge Commission
classification.summary: This act establishes a bridge commission for cities of the first class in Kentucky. The commission is to be appointed by the mayor with the approval of the board of aldermen, consisting of four appointees and the mayor ex-officio. Appointees must be at least twenty-five years old, reside within the city, and not be current city officers or employees. The act specifies terms of appointment, political affiliation balance, and how vacancies are to be filled. The commission is empowered to contract, sue and be sued, and adopt a seal. It also outlines the election of a chairman and the appointment of a secretary and treasurer, along with their salaries.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the formation and powers of a bridge commission in Kentucky cities. It focuses on the appointment process, qualifications of members, terms of service, and the commission's corporate powers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1926.

classification.title: An Act relating to the borrowing of funds by cities.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the mayor of a city to procure loans for the operation and conduct of city business and its departments, pending the full collection of taxes and revenues. These loans are to be repaid from uncollected taxes and revenues. The mayor is empowered to execute promissory notes and pledge uncollected taxes and revenues as security. All borrowed funds are to be applied in the same manner as collected funds. The mayor must report such loans to the treasurer, comptroller, and general council.

590 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
CHAPTER 118 .
AN ACT relating to the qualifications of members and officers of
the police force in cities of the first class , and repealing chap-
ter 84 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1924 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Each member and officer of the police force
hereafter appointed shall be an elector and shall
have been a resident for three years in the city in
which he is appointed . No person shall be appointed
a member or officer of the police force unless he is
well known to be a man of sobriety and integrity ,
and has been and is an orderly and law-abiding citi-
zen . No man shall be appointed a member or officer
of the police force who has been convicted of any
felony , or who has been engaged in any unlawful
calling , or has pursued any calling in a manner for-
bidden by law ; nor shall any person be appointed a
member or officer of said force on account of any
political partisan service rendered by him ; nor shall
any officer or member of said force be removed or
discharged or reduced in grade or pay for any politi-
cal reasons . Their appointment and continuance
upon the police force shall depend absolutely upon
their ability and their willingness to enforce the law :
§ 2. That chapter 84 of the Acts of the General
Assembly of 1924 , and all other laws or parts of laws
in conflict herewith be and the same are now hereby
repealed .
Approved March 3 , 1926 .

602 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section 3783 of the Kentucky Statutes
as amended March 23 , 1920 , chapter 80 , page 376 ,
of the Acts of 1920 , and as further amended March
22 , 1922 , chapter 44 , page 159 , of the Acts of 1922 ,
be and the same is hereby repealed , and in lieu
thereof , the following is enacted :
" Section 3783. Compensation -Cities of the
First and Second Class - Chief Patrolman-Powers .
Patrols shall each be paid by order of the county
court out of the county levy, a sum not to exceed one
dollar for each ten hours they may have been pur-
posely engaged in the performance of their duties ,
except that in counties having cities of the first and
second class , they shall be paid , not more than
$1,800.00 per annum , payable in equal monthly in-
stallments out of the county levy , and the county
court in such counties , having cities of the first and
second class , may designate one of said county
patrols as chief of the county patrolmen, who shall
. serve as such chief , during the pleasure of the county
court, and such chief shall be paid not more than
$2,100.00 per annum out of the county levy , payable
in equal monthly installments. The county court ,
shall by order , fix the amount of their salary within
the limits herein provided . The county court , may
designate one or more of said patrols as motor
patrols , who in the discretion of the fiscal court , may
be provided with motor vehicles .
At a court of claims for the county , the patrols
shall present their claims for services specifying
the number of hours each have been engaged and
verified by the oath of the patrol upon which court
shall fix the amount to be paid .

classification.title: Repeal and Replacement of Section 3783 of Kentucky Statutes Regarding Compensation of Patrols in Cities of First and Second Class
classification.summary: This statute repeals and replaces Section 3783 of the Kentucky Statutes. It establishes compensation for patrols, with specific provisions for counties containing cities of the first and second class. In these counties, patrols are paid up to $1,800 per annum, and a chief patrolman can be designated and paid up to $2,100 per annum. The county court is responsible for fixing salaries and can designate motor patrols. Claims for services must be presented to the court of claims and verified by oath.

teen edition , be repealed and re-enacted and when

CHAPTER 129 .
AN ACT creating a clinic for indigent school children in coun-
ties containing cities of the second class , and authorizing fiscal
courts to levy tax for maintenance of same .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the fiscal court in counties containing cities
of the second class may establish and maintain a
clinic for the use and benefit of the indigent school
children of the county and may levy and collect a
tax not exceeding two cents on the one hundred
($100.00 ) dollars for said purpose .
Said clinic shall be managed and conducted by a
board of trustees consisting of ten ( 10 ) members to
be appointed by the county judge .
They shall serve for a term of four (4 ) years and
without compensation .
Approved March 25 , 1926 .

to the public , to the corporation and to the patrons
of the corporation , and which shall specify the
quality of service to be rendered . Provided : That
if there is no public necessity for the kind of public
utility in question and if the municipality shall
desire to discontinue entirely the kind of service
in question , then this section shall not apply .
$ 2. Each person or corporation desiring to bid
for such franchise shall first deposit with the
proper officer of the municipality , cash or a cer
tified check drawn on a bank of this Commonwealth ,
or on a national bank , equal to five per cent of the
fair estimated cost of the plant required to render
the service , which check or cash shall be forfeited
to the municipality in case the bid should be ac
cepted and the bidder should fail , for thirty days
after the confirmation of the sale , to pay the price
bid and to give a good and sufficient bond in a sum
equal to one-fourth of the fair estimated cost of the
plant to be erected . Said bond shall be so condi-
tioned that it shall be enforceable in case the party
giving it should fail , within a reasonable time , to
establish a suitable plant for rendering the service
and begin rendering the service in the manner set
forth in said terms and conditions ; but this sec-
tion shall not apply to a person or corporation
already owning in such municipality a plant and
equipment sufficient to render the service required
under such franchise .
Approved March 20 , 1926 .

gross neglect as to be the equivalent of fraud , or
who shall be guilty of willful neglect and such forms

ance of the duties required of him by law, or who
shall be guilty of a careless or intentional failure to

duty (the degree of care to be exercised depending

or such gross immorality or misconduct in office , as
amounts to neglect of duty , shall be deemed to be
guilty of neglect of duty within the meaning of this
act ; provided , however , that the definitions herein
contained shall not be construed to be exclusive , as
to the facts , in any particular case arising under this

CHAPTER 143 .
The following title to this act appears in regular form on
printed Senate Bill ( in House ) No. 303 , and is endorsed on the
back of the original typewritten bill , but is omitted from the
enrolled bill from which this chapter is copied . (Editor's Note .)
“AN ACT fixing the salary of sheriffs in counties having an as-
sessment of one hundred million dollars or over , a city of the
second class , and comprising a separate judicial district ; ap-
pointing the number of deputies and clerical assistants and
fixing their salaries ; manner of payment allowing expenses
of office ; manner of payment and providing for the payment
of all monies received by the sheriff to the county treasurer ."
·
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the sheriff of each county having an assessed
value of more than one hundred million dollars and
in which there is located a city of second class , and
which county compromises a separate judicial dis-
trict , shall be paid a salary of five thousand dollars
( $5,000.00 ) per annum . The sheriff shall be per-
mitted to appoint such deputies and clerical assist-
ants , subject to the approval of the fiscal court of
said county , as may be necessary for the proper per-
formance of the duties of the office . The said sheriff
with the approval of said court , shall fix the salaries
of said deputies and assistants but the salaries of
such deputies and clerical assistants shall not in any
year exceed the sum of twenty thousand ( $20,000.00 ) .

CHAPTER 152 .
AN ACT fixing the time when any person convicted for a felony,
may file an application for a parole before the Board of Prison
Commissioners and repealing such laws as may be in conflict
therewith .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. After any prisoner serving a sentence of ten
years or less or sentences aggregating ten years , or
less , has been confined in one of the penal institutions
of this state for one-half of the sentence or sentences
imposed upon such prisoner by the judgment of the
court wherein he was convicted and sentenced , he
may file an application for a parole with the Board
of Prison Commissioners . After any prisoner serv-
ing a sentence or sentences , aggregating more than
ten years and not more than twenty-one years , shall
have been confined in one of the penal institutions
of this state for at least six years of the sentence or
sentences imposed upon such prisoner by the judg-
ment of the court wherein he was convicted and sen-
tenced , he may file an application for parole with the
Board of Prison Commissioners . Persons convicted
for life or persons sentenced for more than twenty-
one years to one of said institutions , shall not be al-
lowed to file an application for parole until eight
years have elapsed , since the said prisoner was con-
fined in said institution .

classification.title: Amendment to Statute Regarding Hard Labor for Fines and Costs
classification.summary: This statute amends existing Kentucky law to increase the daily rate for work performed by defendants to satisfy fines and costs from one dollar to two dollars per day. It also reiterates that the place of work (workhouse, public work, or road) is determined by the county judge, with a preference for road work when weather permits.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the mechanism for working off fines and costs through hard labor. While this system of penal labor was disproportionately applied to Black individuals during the Jim Crow era, the text itself does not contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators. The changes are purely financial (increasing the daily rate) and procedural (reiterating the county judge's role). Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The category is 'incarceration' as it directly relates to the consequences of legal judgments and imprisonment. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an amendment to existing statutes passed by the General Assembly in 1926.

The act also outlines the rate at which prisoners can satisfy their fines ($1.00 per day) and the responsibilities of managers in tracking time and releasing prisoners.
