the Commonwealth or to stay execution of same after such
judgment shall have become final and making it unlawful for
any officer of any county , district , or city of this Common-
wealth to release a prisoner from any county jail while con-
fined therein under a judgment of a court of this Common-
wealth , and fixing penalties for such violations .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section One . It shall be unlawful for any circuit
judge , police judge , or justice of the peace of this
Commonwealth to enter any order in the court over
which such officer presides suspending any judg-
ment or part thereof in favor of the Commonwealth
in a misdemeanor case or staying the execution
thereof after such judgment shall have become
final.
Section Two . It shall be unlawful for any officer
for any county , district , or city of this Common-
wealth to release from any county jail of any
county in the Commonwealth any prisoner who has
been placed in such jail under a final judgment of
a court rendered in favor of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky , prior to the day such judgment shall
have been satisfied , unless such judgment has been
appealed from and superseded , or paid , or re-
plevied , or unless such prisoner so confined in such
a jail shall have been pardoned by the Governor of
this Commonwealth or released on a judgment
under a writ of habeas corpus . Provided however ,
any regular circuit court judge of this Common-
wealth may , during term time or during vacation ,
enter an order on the records of the circuit court

classification.summary: This act prohibits circuit judges, police judges, and justices of the peace from suspending judgments or staying executions in misdemeanor cases in favor of the Commonwealth after they have become final. It also makes it unlawful for any county, district, or city officer to release a prisoner from jail who is held under a final judgment for the Commonwealth, unless the judgment has been appealed, superseded, paid, replevied, or the prisoner has been pardoned or released via habeas corpus. Regular circuit court judges are granted specific authority to enter orders during term or vacation.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the authority of judges and court officers regarding the suspension of judgments and the release of prisoners. It focuses on procedural aspects of the legal system and does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines the scope of the law without any racial undertones. classification.title: An Act relating to circuit judges and the suspension of fines and jail sentences.
classification.summary: This statute allows circuit judges to suspend fines or jail sentences for misdemeanor convictions under specific circumstances. These circumstances include the endangerment of the prisoner's life or the life of another prisoner due to the physical condition of the prisoner to be paroled, or if the prisoner has served six or more consecutive months in jail for misdemeanors, unpaid fines, or failure to execute a peace bond. The parole is granted during good behavior and can be revoked within twelve months.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the discretionary powers of circuit judges to grant paroles for misdemeanor convictions. It outlines conditions related to the prisoner's health, length of confinement, and financial penalties. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as an act from the General Assembly of 1926.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That chapter 72 of the acts of the General As-
sembly of 1922 , approved March 23 , 1922 , be
amended and re -enacted so as to read as follows :
§ 1. No person , firm or corporation shall sell ,
offer or expose for sale , or traffic in , any egg unfit for
human food , unless the same is broken in shell and
then denatured so that it can not be used for human
food . For the purpose of this act , an egg shall be
deemed unfit for human food if it be addled or moldy ,
a black rot , a white rot , or a blood ring , or if it has
an adherent yolk , or a bloody or green white ; or if it
be incubated beyond the blood ring stage ; or if it
consists in whole or in part of a filthy , decomposed
or putrid substance .
§ 2. Between May 15th of each year and Jan-
uary 15th of the following year , no person , firm or
corporation shall, in buying or selling eggs , take or
give a greater or less dockage for eggs unfit for food
as defined in section 1 of this act than the actual
dockage which has been determined by the careful
candling of the eggs so purchased or sold , and said
person , firm or corporation shall keep such candling
records as may be required by the rules and regula-
tions . All such records shall be open at all times
reasonable for examination by the State Board of
Health , or its official representative .
§ 3. The term " candling " as used herein , shall
be construed to mean the careful examination , in a
partially dark room or place , of the whole egg by
means of a strong light , covered by an opaque con-
tainer or shield having not more than two apertures ,
each aperture not to exceed one and one -quarter inch
in diameter , nor be less than one-half inch in di-

It specifies how additional board members are appointed in cases of significant gifts, grants, or devises, and outlines the appointment process for trustees by mayors of cities of the second class, based on lists from the State Board of Health. The terms and qualifications of these trustees are also addressed.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the formation and governance of tuberculosis districts and the appointment of trustees. It details procedures for appointing trustees based on gifts, grants, or devises, and specifically outlines how mayors of cities of the second class appoint trustees from lists provided by the State Board of Health. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Organization of Tuberculosis Sanatorium Districts and the Appointment of Trustees Thereof," Approved March 17, 1914, and All Acts Amendatory Thereto.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the organization of tuberculosis sanatorium districts and the appointment of trustees. It allows for the increase of the number of trustees if a district receives a significant gift or grant ($100,000 or more) with the condition that the donor can nominate additional trustees. The appointment of these additional trustees is handled by the county judge.

CHAPTER 160 .
AN ACT authorizing the restoration to sound mind , of persons
who have been adjudged of unsound mind , or who may be here-
after adjudged of unsound mind , so that persons who have been
adjudged of unsound mind can , by proper judgment be restored
and given all the rights of persons of sound mind .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Whenever it shall appear to the county or
circuit court, from an affidavit filed , in which any
person has been adjudged of unsound mind , that a
person of unsound mind has been restored to his
proper senses , or that the inquest was false or
fraudulent the court shall forthwith direct the facts
to be inquired into by a jury in open court , and make
all necessary orders and decrees in the premises . If
the jury shall find the person charged who had here-
tofore been found of unsound mind, to be of sound
mind then the court shall enter an order and judg-
ment restoring said person to his proper senses , and
giving him all the rights and privileges of a person
of sound mind .
§ 2. The proceedings instituted under this act
may be by an affidavit filed by the person under in-
vestigation , or by his next friend , and if the person ,
so charged , has a committee the committee shall be
notified of said proceedings by reasonable notice in
writing , and the county attorney shall also be noti-
fied of said proceedings .
§ 3. The proceedings for restoration of the per-
son to sound mind , who has been found of unsound
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 782

classification.title: AN ACT authorizing the restoration to sound mind, of persons who have been adjudged of unsound mind, or who may be hereafter adjudged of unsound mind, so that persons who have been adjudged of unsound mind can, by proper judgment be restored and given all the rights of persons of sound mind.
classification.summary: This act outlines the legal process for restoring the rights of individuals who have been previously adjudged of unsound mind. It allows for a jury trial to determine if a person has regained sound mind, and if so, the court will issue an order restoring them to all rights and privileges of a sound-minded person. Proceedings can be initiated by the individual, their next friend, or with notification to their committee and the county attorney.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for appeals from assessments of boards of supervisors and equalizers in cities and towns of the fourth, fifth and sixth classes.
classification.summary: This act allows any taxpayer who is aggrieved by the assessment or valuation of their property by a board of supervisors or equalizers in a city or town of the fourth, fifth, or sixth class to appeal to the quarterly court of the county within thirty days of the board's final adjournment. The taxpayer must file a certified copy of the board's action with the judge of the quarterly court. The act also specifies a fee for the clerk to provide this copy.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to repeal and re-enact section four thousand eighty-four of Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's edition of nineteen hundred fifteen, relating to revenue and taxation, defining duties of the Auditor of Public Accounts as to certification of franchise and tangible assessments of corporations liable to local taxation.
classification.summary: This act repeals and re-enacts Section 4084 of the Kentucky Statutes (1915 edition) concerning revenue and taxation. It clarifies the Auditor of Public Accounts' duty to certify to county clerks the portion of corporate franchise and tangible assessments liable for local taxation (county, city, town, or district). The county clerk is then responsible for filing this certificate and forwarding it to the appropriate collecting officer.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation ; providing for the re- peal , amendment and re-enactment of section 4102 of Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's 1915 edition ; relating to the taxation of the property of railroads and bridge companies ; and repealing existing statutes .
classification.summary: This act amends Section 4102 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's 1915 edition) concerning the taxation of railroad and bridge companies. It establishes that the same rate of taxation levied on other real estate will apply to the value of railroad bridges and real estate, as well as to the portion of railroad or bridge property located within cities, towns, or tax districts. The act also specifies how the mileage of railroads will be valued for taxation purposes.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 4258 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Omitted Property and Penalties
classification.summary: This act amends a section of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the assessment of omitted property. It outlines a process for retrospective assessment of various forms of property that were not initially assessed, including a penalty of 100% of the taxes owed plus 6% annual interest. An additional 20% penalty is applied if the assessment is done through specific legal actions. The act also specifies a limitation on when such actions can be commenced, stating no action can be taken for property assessed as of September 1, 1917. It also includes provisions for a referendum on the act.

It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to an amendment to existing statutes passed by the General Assembly in 1926. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about tax law and lacks any racial component. No human review is needed.

CHAPTER 165 .
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation ; imposing an excise or
license tax on all distilled spirits known as whiskey , brandy ,
gin , rum or other species of double stamp spirits sold at re-
tail in this state ; requiring every individual , association ,
partnership , joint stock company and corporation selling such
distilled spirits at retail to pay said tax for the privilege of
engaging in said business ; providing for monthly reports for
the purpose of ascertaining the amount of tax due ; providing
for monthly payments of the amount of license tax due ; fixing
of penalty for failure to make such monthly report and settle-
ment ; providing for the manner of distribution of the tax so
collected ; repealing all other license and excise taxes on the
businesses covered by this act and declaring an emergency to
exist .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Every individual , association , partnership ,
joint stock company or corporation engaged in the
business of selling at retail distilled spirits known
as whiskey , brandy, gin, rum or other species of
double stamp spirits in this state shall pay a license
or excise tax to the Commonwealth of Kentucky of
fifty cents on each pint so sold , and the said tax so
collected shall be used for the general expenses of

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 755
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That an annual ad valorem tax of fifty cents
on each one hundred dollars of the fair cash value
of all unmanufactured agricultural products , sub-
ject to taxation , shall be paid by the owner , person
or corporation assessed , which tax shall be for state
purposes only, and in lieu of all other ad valorem
taxes by the State , or any county , city , town or other
taxing district , on such unmanufactured agricul-
tural products wherever grown except that cities
and counties of the state may each impose an ad
valorem tax of not exceeding fifteen cents on each
one hundred dollars of the fair cash value of all
such unmanufactured agricultural products subject
to taxation within their limits , provided that un-
manufactured agricultural products actually on
hand at the plants of manufacturing concerns for
the purpose of manufacture , and unmanufactured
agricultural products in the hands of the producer
or in the hands of any agent or agency of the pro-
ducer to which said products have been conveyed or
assigned for the purpose of sale by the producer
shall be subject to State taxation only .
2. All state taxes collected hereunder shall be
paid into the State treasury to the credit of the gen-
eral expenditure fund .
§ 3. The assessment , the date thereof, the equali-
zation and the collection of the tax herein imposed
shall be governed by the laws applicable to other
tangible personal property .
4. In event a referendum is desired upon this
act , then such referendum shall be conducted in all
things in the manner provided in chapter eight of
the Acts of the special one thousand nine hundred
and seventeen session of the General Assembly.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 802

classification.title: An Act relating to taxation of unmanufactured agricultural products.
classification.summary: This act establishes an annual ad valorem tax of fifty cents per one hundred dollars on the fair cash value of unmanufactured agricultural products for state purposes only. It specifies that this tax is in lieu of all other ad valorem taxes by the state or any taxing district. However, cities and counties can impose an additional ad valorem tax of up to fifteen cents. The act also exempts unmanufactured agricultural products on hand at manufacturing plants or in the hands of the producer or their agent for sale from local taxation, subjecting them only to state taxation. The collection and assessment of this tax will be governed by existing laws for tangible personal property. A referendum provision is also included.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the taxation of agricultural products. It does not mention race, nor does it establish any differential treatment based on race. The provisions are economic in nature, related to tax rates and exemptions for agricultural goods. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' from 1926.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-

1. The fiscal affairs of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky shall be administered by the fiscal court
under a uniform budget system . The county budget
shall provide for all the funds to be expended by the
county from current revenues for each fiscal year .
All funds of the county provided by bond issues
shall be appropriated and accounted for under the
provisions of this act .

commission . The county budget commission shall

two members to be appointed by the county judge ,
by an order entered on the county court order book .

The fiscal court shall be a court of record and
shall hold three regular terms in each year . The first
regular term to be held on the first Tuesday of
April ; and the second term to be held on the first
Tuesday of October ; and the third term to be held
during the month of December , on a date to be fixed
by the fiscal court by order entered upon the fiscal
court order book . At the term to be held in Decem-
ber the fiscal court shall adopt a county budget and
make the county levy for the next fiscal year.
The fiscal court may by order fix a different
date for the terms to be held in April and October ,
but the court has no power to adopt a county budget
or make the county levy in any month other than the
month of December .
The county judge shall have power to call spe-
cial terms of the fiscal court for the transaction of
any business of which the fiscal court has jurisdic-
tion . Whenever the necessity exists for a special
term of the fiscal court, and if county judge is un-
able to act , or refuses to call said special term , the
special term may be called by a written notice given
to each member of the fiscal court, but said notice
must be signed by a majority of the members
thereof .

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 4399a-10 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, Relating to the Levy of Taxes for Common School Purposes.
classification.summary: This act details the process for levying taxes for common school purposes in Kentucky. It outlines the duties of the fiscal court, county tax commissioner, county court clerk, and county board of education in assessing property, preparing school budgets, and determining tax rates. The act specifies that taxes will be levied on taxable property subject to state taxation, excluding property within graded school districts and cities/towns with separate school systems. It also sets a minimum and maximum tax rate per one hundred dollars of taxable property.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the financial and administrative aspects of common school funding in Kentucky. It outlines the responsibilities of various county officials in levying taxes and preparing school budgets. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The exclusion of property in graded school districts and cities/towns maintaining separate school systems refers to administrative divisions, not racial ones. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1926, page 860
classification.summary: This statute mandates that the fiscal court impose a capitation tax not exceeding one dollar on all legal male voters residing in the territory affected by an ad valorem tax for the county board of education. The sheriff is responsible for collecting this tax, along with other state and county revenues, and remitting it to the county treasurer, who serves as the ex-officio treasurer for the county board of education. The sheriff's commission for collecting this specific school tax is limited to one percent of the total school tax collected, and the commission is calculated proportionally based on the combined funds collected.
classification.reasoning: The statute imposes a capitation tax on 'legal male voters'. Historically, in the Jim Crow South, poll taxes and capitation taxes were often used as a mechanism to disenfranchise Black voters. While this statute does not explicitly mention race, the imposition of a poll tax on 'male voters' in 1926 Kentucky strongly suggests an implicit racial component, as such taxes were a common tool for voter suppression against African Americans. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with an implicit racial indicator. The confidence is set at 0.7 because while the mechanism is a known Jim Crow tactic, the statute itself doesn't explicitly name race, requiring human review to confirm the historical context of its application.

officials charged with the collection or disbursement
of public money shall be required to report annually
to the State Inspector and Examiner of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , the total receipts and disburse-
ments of each of their several offices or departments .
The system so adopted shall require each county
treasurer in all counties of the Commonwealth , and
all other officers of the county who receive or dis-
burse county or state funds , to keep an accurate ac-
count of receipts and disbursements , so as to show a
daily balance of receipts and disbursements , and to
conform to the laws governing the fiscal administra-
tion of State and county governments . Said system
shall be so constructed that all county officials hand-
ling State funds shall be required to report annually
to the State Inspector and Examiner of the Com-
monwealth , showing receipts and disbursements and
such other financial statements as may be required
by the said State Inspector and Examiner .

by the Auditor of Public Accounts , and from the fis-

be produced by each sheriff or collector to the county
court on or before that day , and no tax book shall be
delivered to the sheriff or collector after the first
year of his term who shall fail to exhibit such quietus
in or before that date . He may execute bond at any
time after he receives his certificate of election up
to and including the first day of March succeeding
his election , and it shall be the duty of the judge of
the county court to hold a court at any time the
sheriff may request for that purpose . The county
judge shall judge of the sufficiency of the surety , and
in no case shall sureties be taken who are not jointly
worth , subject to execution after the payments of all
their debts and liabilities , a sum equal to the aggre-
gate amount of money , which may probably be re-
ceived by the sheriff or collector during the year suc-
ceeding the execution of the bond . The Common-
wealth , the county and taxing district shall have a
lien from the date the sheriff begins to act upon the
.

lected for the state into the state treasury by the

Sec . 4146. Sheriff's Settlements ; Exceptions to ;
Appeal from Judgment on.- Each sheriff or col-
lector shall, when required by the fiscal court , settle
his accounts of county or district taxes , and at the
regular October term of each year the fiscal court
shall appoint some competent person , other than the
Commonwealth's or county attorney , to settle the
accounts of the sheriff or collector of money due the
county or district . The report of such settlement
shall be filed in the county clerk's office and be sub-
ject to exceptions by the sheriff or collector or county
attorney who shall represent the Commonwealth and
county , and the county court shall try and determine
such exceptions . An appeal may be prosecuted by
either party from the judgment of the county court
on such settlement , in the same manner as provided
by law for appeals from judgments of the quarterly
court , except that the county attorney shall not be
required to give an appeal bond , or actions may be
instituted in any court of competent jurisdiction to
correct the settlement ; and the settlement , when ap-
proved, shall be recorded in the county clerk's office .

Section Eight . Transfer Tax Upon the Personal
Property of Non -Resident Decedents . One . All

within the jurisdiction of the state and any interest

erty belonging to a decedent non -resident shall be
found to be physically located in Kentucky , it shall
be the duty of the State Tax Commission , to so in-
form the state official collecting the inheritance tax
in the state of domicile of the said decedent provided
that said state furnishes like information to the Ken-
tucky State Tax Commission in a reciprocal manner .
Three . All moneys so collected shall be distributed
in like manner as those collected under the resident
inheritance tax act .
Section 3. All laws and parts of laws in conflict
herewith are hereby repealed .
Approved March 19, 1926 .

erties .

monwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. State Purchasing Commission Created . A
central purchasing agency for the Commonwealth of
Kentucky , to be known as the State Purchasing
Commission , is hereby created . The said commis-
sion shall consist of three members as hereinafter
provided . The said commission is hereby created
a corporation , with power to contract and be con-
tracted with , to sue and be sued , and to do and per-
form all things requisite and necessary to execute
the duties and powers hereinafter conferred upon
it. The said commission may have and use a com-
mon seal , and is hereby empowered to adopt rules ,
regulations or by-laws for the government of the
commission not inconsistent with the Constitution
or the laws of the Commonwealth .
§ 2. Members of the Commission . The chairman
of the State Purchasing Commission shall be ap-
pointed by the Governor for a term from the date
of his appointment , shall be a person of experience
and recognized ability in the purchase of merchan-
dise , shall hold office at the pleasure of the Governor
and shall devote his entire time to the performance
of his duties under this act . The chairman shall be
a qualified elector of this State and a person of rec-
ognized business qualifications and fitness for the
purposes contemplated by this act , and shall receive
as compensation for his services the sum of five
thousand dollars ( $5,000 ) per annum . The Com-
missioner of Public Institutions and one of the
members of the State Highway Commission to be
designated by the Governor shall, ex- officio , consti-

operation or maintenance of buildings , roads and
highways, and it shall be the duty of all persons ,
boards , and commissions , charged with such respon-
sibility , to procure such information from the State
Purchasing Commission , before submitting any
estimates or requests for the purchase of material
or supplies for the construction of any buildings or
roads or for the maintenance and operation of any
State department , board , commission or other office .
§ 13. Existing Contracts not Affected by this
Act . The provisions of this act shall not apply to
contracts entered into and existing prior to the pas-
sage and approval of this act nor the Kentucky Uni-
versity or State Normal Schools for foods , but said
institutions shall make monthly reports to the Com-
mission .
§ 14. Transfer of Personal Property from One
Department to Another . The State Purchasing
Commission shall have authority , when there is a
surplus of tools , implements , machinery , equipment
or other supplies in one department , and the same
is not necessary for the use of said department , and
would be necessary for the use of another or addi-
tional department of the State , to transfer said
tools , implements , machinery , equipment or other
supplies to the department having need of the same ,
either temporarily or permanently , and if perman-
ently said Commission shall take into account such
transfer of property in compiling estimates for the
biennial budget , insofar as they may pertain to the
departments affected by the transfer .

entries.ocr_text:
862 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
CHAPTER 182 .
AN ACT to prohibit the owner of real property from failing to
apply the proceeds of the sale thereof or loan thereon to the
payment of the amounts owing for labor or materials used in
the erection , repair or improvement of any structure thereon ,
and prescribing a penalty for the violation thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. If the owner of any legal or equitable title
or interest in land or in the improvements thereon
shall contract for any labor or material used in the
erection , repair or improvement of any structure
thereon , under such circumstances that a lien for
the payment therefor has or may attach to said
property , and shall sell or mortgage same before
the expiration of the time provided by law for the
filing and recording of a mechanic's lien or material-
men's lien thereon , he shall when he received the
consideration for said sale or the proceeds of said
loan pay in full such sum or sums as may be owing
for said labor and materials , unless released in writ-
ing thereupon by the person or persons furnishing
the labor or materials . If said consideration or the
proceeds of said loan shall not be great enough to
make such payment in full, then he shall make pro
rata payments on all claims for such labor and ma-
terial to the persons entitled to said payments , or
either or any of them .
§ 2. Any person or corporation who shall violate
any of the provisions of this act shall be guilty of
a misdemeanor , and upon conviction thereof shall
be fined not less than $50.00 or more than $ 1,000.00 ,
or be imprisoned not less than one nor more than
twelve months , or both so fined and imprisoned in
the discretion of the jury .

CHAPTER 183 .
AN ACT to require persons building , repairing or improving
property for others to apply money received from the owner
to the payment of bills for labor or materials and providing
a penalty for the violation thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. When any payment shall be made by the
owner of any property to any contractor , builder ,
architect or other person who shall build , repair or
improve said property for another , under such cir ·
cumstances that a mechanic's or materialmen's lien
may be imposed on said property , said contractor ,
builder , architect or other person shall from the
proceeds of such payment pay to all persons who
have furnished material or performed labor on said
work , the full amount of said material or labor .
2. If any payment by said owner to said con-
tractor , builder , architect or other person is not
great enough to pay in full all such bills for material
and labor , then said contractor , builder , architect or
other person shall , from such proceeds , pay said
claims pro rata to the amount of the payment or
payments made to him, unless otherwise agreed be-
tween said contractor , builder , architect or other
person and the holder of such claim for material or
labor .
$ 3. This act shall not apply in those cases where
persons furnishing material or performing labor
shall have waived in writing their right to file me-
chanic's or materialmen's liens on said property .

Whereas, said counties are losing the benefits of said sand and
gravel deposits because of there being no law at the present

deposits for development ; now , therefore ,

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the fiscal court or county commis-
sioners of each of the counties of the State may ,
after first adopting the provisions of this act , estab-
lish a system of old age pensions in accordance with
the provisions provided herein and may levy , collect
and disburse such sums of money from the general
funds of their county as may be necessary to comply
with the provisions of this act . After having oper-
ated under such system for one ( 1 ) year or more ,
any county may abandon such system .
§ 2. Any person while residing in a county which
maintains a system of old age pensions , who shall
comply with the provisions of this act shall be en-
titled to a pension in old age . The amount of such
pension shall be fixed by the county judge with due
regard to the conditions in each case , but in no case
shall it exceed two hundred and fifty ($250.00 ) dol-
lars per annum .

an applicant who has attained the age of seventy
(70 ) years or upwards , and has been a citizen of the
United States for at least fifteen ( 15 ) years before
making application for a pension and has resided
in the State and county in which he makes applica-
tion for at least ten ( 10 ) years immediately preced-
ing the date of application .

age pension who is a professional beggar or who , by
manual labor or by his or her skill or knowledge in

CHAPTER 193.
AN ACT to amend sections 2, 42 , 55 , 59 and 74 of an act entitled
"An Act to provide an elective system of workmen's compensa-
tion for industrial accidents ," and known as chapter 33 of
the Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky for the year 1916 , and subsequent amendments
thereto , said sections as amended and to be amended being
known as sections 4881 , 4923 , 4938 , 4942 , and 4957 of Carroll's
Kentucky Statutes , sixth edition , 1922 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section 2 of said act be repealed and
re - enacted so that when re - enacted said section shall
read as follows :
"The term ' employer ' as used in this act shall be
construed to include municipal corporations and any
subdivision or corporations thereof , and any election
with reference to this act shall be exercised by the
law-making or other governing body thereof ; and
the term ' employer ' as used in this act shall also be
construed to include all departments of the state
government , and any election with reference to this
act shall be exercised by the head of such depart-
ment so electing ; provided , however , that nothing
contained in this act shall be construed as amending
or repealing any statute or ordinance relating to
associations or funds for the relief , pensioning ,
retirement or other benefit of any employee of such
municipal employer , or any of the employees of any
state department , or the widows , children or depen-
dents of such employees , or in any manner interfer-
ing with the same as now or hereafter established . "

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the State Highway Commission of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky shall have the power ,
whenever in its judgment it is necessary for the
safety of the traveling public to do so , to order any
railroad company , either steam or electric , owning
or operating a railroad in this Commonwealth , to
eliminate any grade crossing where any of the pri-
mary roads of the State cross at grade the railroad
tracks of such railroad company , shall have the
power to determine the location of the crossing to
be substituted , the grade thereof, and whether it
shall pass over or under the railroad track or tracks .
§ 2. Whenever in the judgment of the State
Highway Commission it deems it desirable in the
interest of safety of the traveling public that any
grade crossing on any of the State primary roads
should be eliminated , or existing overhead or under-
pass structures changed , the State Highway Com-
mission shall give at least ten days ' notice by regis-
tered mail to the railroad company of a hearing to
be held at a time and place stated in said notice , at
which hearing it shall consider whether or not the
proposed grade separation or change is reasonably
necessary for the safety of the traveling public and
the most advantageous method of effecting said
grade separation or change . Thereupon, if the judg-
ment of the State Highway Commission be that the
grade separation or change is reasonably necessary
for the safety of the traveling public it shall make
an order to that effect and furnish a copy thereof
by registered mail to the railroad company . Said
order shall specify the location of the crossing to be
substituted , the grade thereof , whether it shall pass
over or under the railroad tracks of the railroad

CHAPTER 195 .
AN ACT to amend and re -enact sections 1, 2 and 3 of chapter
290 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1924 , approved
March 7 , 1924 , relating to roads and bridges and the State
Highway Department ; creating a State Highway Commission
and fixing their qualifications , duties and compensation ; pro-
viding for the appointment of an executive inspector for said
commission and fixing his compensation , changing the name of
said department ; providing for appointees and employees and
compensation of same ; and declaring an emergency .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section 1 of chapter 290 of the Acts of
the General Assembly of 1924 , relating to roads and
bridges , approved March 7 , 1924 , be and the same is
hereby amended and re -enacted so that when
amended and re -enacted it will read as follows :
The department of the state government formerly
known as the Department of Public Roads and
Department of State Roads and Highways is here-
by continued and shall be known as the State High-
way Department , and the State Highway Commis-
sion hereinafter provided for shall be an agent of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky in connection with
the construction , reconstruction and maintenance of
the state roads and bridges as hereinafter provided ,
and such other duties as said commission is required
to perform , and the acts of said commission shall be
deemed to be the acts of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky in performing the duties hereby imposed .

classification.summary: This statute outlines the composition, qualifications, and duties of a State Highway Commission. It specifies that members must be citizens and residents of Kentucky, at least thirty years of age, and representative businessmen knowledgeable about road and highway needs. classification.summary: This act establishes a commission to regulate bridge and ferry companies operating toll bridges or ferries for the transportation of persons over streams that are part of the state primary road system. CHAPTER 336
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 1
Resolution providing for extra help for Senate .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the Senate of Kentucky do now proceed to
elect the following persons for extra help , to be
paid the salaries set opposite their respective
names . Said salaries to be paid out on warrants of

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1007
CHAPTER 337
SENATE RESOLUTION NO . 2
Joint Resolution providing for extra help for Lieutenant Gov-
ernor and Chief Clerk of Senate and House .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the President of the Senate is hereby au-
thorized to employ a personal page at three dollars
per day and he is further.authorized to employ a
secretary at a salary of eight dollars per day and he
is further authorized to employ a stenographer for
the Chief Clerk at a salary of eight dollars per day .
The Chief Clerk shall issue vouchers to the persons
entitled to same , and the Auditor of Public Accounts
is directed to draw his warrant on the Treasurer in
payment thereof out of any funds in his hands un-
appropriated .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 338
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 3
Resolution providing a contingent fund for the clerk of the
House and the clerk of the Senate .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That for the purpose of paying such expenses
as the Chief Clerk of the Senate of Kentucky is di
rected to incur by the Senate of Kentucky , there is
hereby appropriated out of the general revenues of
the State , as a contingent fund , the sum of five hun-
dred dollars ( $ 500 ) and for the purpose of paying
the expenses incurred by the Chief Clerk of the
House of Representatives of the Commonwealth ,

CHAPTER 339

and the Acts of the General Assembly of one thou-

entries.ocr_text:
1010 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
sively that of the State to exercise independently , we confess
to serious concern for the future of both the nation as a whole
and the separate Commonwealths comprising it if the far
reaching principles involved in the federal revenue bill now
pending before Congress , particularly with reference to the
inheritance tax feature thereof , providing a joint levy to be
assessed by the United States government and divided as be-
tween the federal government and the state , shall be disre-
garded by the legislative bodies of the American Common-
wealths . It is maintained that a state should not be compelled in
this fashion to become a party to a joint tax levy under penalty
of losing a source of its revenue if it dissents . The purpose
of the proposed law is ulterior . Never before in the history
of the United States except under stress of war conditions has
the federal government ever attempted to levy a property tax .
If the present attempt is accomplished the danger will be im-
minent of the federal government next dictating the entire
taxing policies of all the states . We hold that the rights of
the states , although often disregarded , still remain one of the
fundamental principles of our Democratic government , and
that if we continue to surrender to the federal government
every function which belongs to the states , it will be only a
question of time before centralization , with its attendant and
inevitable autocracy , will submerge every remaining vestige
of state sovereignty . The inheritance tax is a direct property
tax, and to assume the authority to assess a joint levy , the
federal government coming into the state and arbitrarily fix-
ing the rate of assessment and sharing in the revenues , leav-
ing to the state no election of choice , is , we hold , an unwar-
ranted invasion , economically as well as politically , of the
sacred principle of self-determination which , if permitted to
go unchallenged , will prove only the forerunner of even further
abrogations of the prerogative which the states have enjoyed
without abridgment since the formation of the American
union upon the bedrock foundation of democracy ; therefore ,
Be it resolved that the Senate of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , the House of Representatives
Concurring :
Respectfully enters its protest against the pas-
sage of this provision by the Congress of the United
States .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 1057

CHAPTER 351
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 18
Resolution on the death of W. L. Mapother.
Resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , the House of
Representatives concurring :
That the General Assembly has heard with pro-
found regret of the death of Wible L. Mapother ,
president of the Louisville & Nashville Railroad
system , and one of the most prominent and valuable
citizens of the Commonwealth . Entering the employ
of that corporation when a mere boy as filing clerk
in the president's office , he climbed his way up
through successive steps to the office of vice -presi-
dent and assistant to the president , the late Milton
H. Smith , one of the great railroad builders of the
country, and upon his death , to the head of the sys-
tem . Ascending from filing clerk to the vice-presi-
dency when only thirty -two years of age and to the
presidency at forty-nine , beside serving as Regional
Director of Railroads during the World's War , and
dying when fifty -three years of age , marks his ca-
reer as one of the most remarkable in the history of
the State of which he was a native son . In his death ,
the State has lost a worthy , public spirited citizen
and a man of great constructive and executive abil-
ity ; the railroad world one of its most conspicuous
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1926, p. 1069

CHAPTER 354
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 23
Joint Resolution relative to the Clinchfield Railway lease , finance
docket , five thousand three hundred and twenty-two of the
Interstate Commerce Commission , Washington , D. C.
Whereas , the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company has
filed with the Interstate Commerce Commission and the Railroad
Commission of Kentucky and Virginia , an application requesting
a certificate of necessity and public convenience , wherein they
seek to modify the condition contained in the lease recently
authorized by the Interstate Commerce Commission for it to
acquire control of the Clinchfield Railway ; and
Whereas , it was one of the stipulated conditions upon which
said lease was authorized , that the said Louisville and Nashville
Railroad Company build connecting links between its Lexington
and Eastern Division at McRoberts , Kentucky , and Elkhorn City ,
Kentucky , the terminus of the Clinchfield Railway and its
Cumberland Valley Division with the Clinchfield Railway at
some point convenient therefor ; and
Whereas , the failure of the said Louisville and Nashville Rail-
road Company to comply with the original agreement and stip-
ulation in said order authorizing said lease will continue the
isolation of the Eastern Kentucky coal fields from southern and
eastern markets , will create dissension , by reason of its failure
to remedy the present unfair competitive situation , will imperil
the investment of many millions of dollars in said coal industry ,
will fail to relieve the present intolerable system of supplying
empty coal cars to said coal fields , and will cause irreparable
loss to both public and private interests throughout the State
who have relied on the proposals of said railroad company to
make said connection ; therefore ,

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1027
Be it resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , the
House of Representatives concurring therein :
That it is the sense of this General Assembly that
the Interstate Commerce Commission and the Rail-
road Commissions of Kentucky and Virginia should
refuse any and all applications and requests of the
said Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company for
any modification of said lease in so far as it would
relieve the said railroad company from making its
proposed connection specified in said lease , and
especially that part of same from McRoberts , Ken-
tucky , to Elkhorn City , Kentucky , the terminus of
the Clinchfield Railway .
Be it further resolved , that a copy of this resolu-
tion be transmitted to the Interstate Commerce
Commission , Washington , D. C. , to the State Rail-
road Commission , Frankfort , Kentucky , and to the
Railroad Commission of Virginia , Richmond , Vir-
ginia , bringing to their attention the irreparable
damage to Eastern Kentucky if said application is
granted .
Approved March 8, 1926 .

CHAPTER 356
SENATE JOINT RESOLUTION No. 25
Preamble and Joint Resolution in relation to the erection of a
suitable monument over the remains of Governor James Clark .
Whereas , the preservation in honor of the memory of their
illustrious dead is a sacred duty of all republics , imposed alike
by a just pride and sound policy , and
Whereas , Kentucky has failed to distinguish by a proper
monument the burial place of the remains of Governor James
Clark , one of her noblest citizens and once her chief magistrate ,
eminent as a statesman and jurist ; and
Whereas , the legislature in eighteen hundred and fifty -six
passed a resolution and an appropriation providing for the re-
moval of the remains of Governor Clark to the Frankfort ceme-
tery and erecting a suitable monument over his remains ; and
Whereas , the said resolution was never carried out , and
Whereas , it is probably now too late to remove the dust of
the illustrious Governor from its present resting place on the
beautiful eminence near his old historic home overlooking the
city of Winchester ; and

CHAPTER 363
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 38
Declaring it the sense of the General Assembly of the State of
Kentucky that unjust and discriminating freight rates should
not be imposed upon shipments of coal from Kentucky mining
districts .
Whereas there are pending before the Interstate Commerce
Commission petitions filed by coal shippers of the Pennsylvania ,
Ohio , Indiana and Illinois mining districts seeking to further in-
crease the spread or differences in freight rates to their interest
and to the disadvantage and detriment of Kentucky coals ; and
Whereas, the existing freight rates on coal shipments from
the mining districts of Kentucky are already in excess of rates
from Pennsylvania , Ohio , Indiana and Illinois districts , and are
discriminatory against the shippers of Kentucky coal and a
further increase of said freight rates would place additional un-
reasonable burdens on the coal industry of this state ; and
Whereas , approximately forty million tons of coal are shipped
annually from Kentucky , practically all of which is sold in keen
competition with the coals from Pennsylvania , Ohio , Indiana and
Illinois , the imposition of higher freight rates or further increase
in the spread or differences in said freight rates would largely
eliminate Kentucky coal from competition in the markets of the
north , northwest , east and northeast ; therefore ,
Be it resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , the
House of Representatives concurring therein :
That any further increase of the differentials in
freight rates for the transportation of Kentucky
coal to the consuming markets would mean economic
disaster to the coal industry of Kentucky . That
the suspension of Kentucky mines would be attended
by the consequent unemployment of labor both in
the mining industry and also railway employes and

CHAPTER 365
HOUSE RESOLUTION No. 5
Resolution for the benefit of D. Owen Robinson , of Frankfort ,
Kentucky .
Whereas , on the 14th day of August , 1884 , D. Owen Robinson ,
who was then a guard , employed by the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky, at the penitentiary in Frankfort , Kentucky , whilst in the ,
faithful performance of his duty as such guard was attacked
and shot by one George Alsop , a prisoner in said penitentiary ,
and
Whereas , said pistol took effect in said Robinson's hip , shat-
tering the bone , disabling and incapacitating him from labor for
the remainder of his life ; and
Whereas , said Robinson is now 78 years of age , and is now ,
and has been for many years unable to work for his support and
livelihood , it is now the expressed sense of the General Assembly
that said Robinson should be compensated for said injury and
resultant loss of earning power .
Be it therefore resolved by the General Assembly
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Auditor of the Commonwealth be
and he is now directed to draw his warrant for fifty
( $50.00 ) dollars per month , payable to said D. Owen
Robinson, and the Treasurer is directed to honor
said and such warrant and pay same to said D.
Owen Robinson , as long as he, the said Robinson ,
may live, or until the foregoing resolution be nulli-
fied .
Approved March 23 , 1926 .

CHAPTER 366
HOUSE RESOLUTION NO . 11
Joint Resolution providing for the payment of assistants for the
Superintendent of Public Printing .
Whereas , during the session of the General Assembly it has
been impossible for the Superintendent of Public Printing , un-
aided , to expeditiously look after the printing of bills and reso-
lutions , and
Whereas , during the present session of the General Assembly ,
he has had in his office two clerks , one for the House and one
for the Senate elected by the Board of Printing Commissioners ,
assisting him in said work and in other printing incidental to
the General Assembly ; therefore ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Joe D. Bradburn , Superintendent of Public
'Printing , be allowed the sum of seven hundred fifty
dollars ( $750.00 ) to be expended by him in the pay-
ment of help in the care of all printed bills and other
matter pertaining to printing during the present
session of the General Assembly, said services when
obtained to terminate when this General Assembly
adjourns , and the Auditor of Public Accounts shall
draw his warrant upon the Treasurer for payment
of said sum .
Approved March 20 , 1926 .
